results.title: Lattics vs. Scrivener: A Modern Choice for Today’s Creators | Lattics Review
results.content:
Real-world scenario: When writing a multi-perspective novel, in Scrivener you have to jump between different folders, trying to remember each character's story progress. In Lattics, you open the plotline view, and four parallel timelines are clearly displayed, allowing you to see at a glance which character's plot is lagging and where there are timeline conflicts.

3. Modern and User-Friendly: From Complex Tools to Intuitive Experience Scrivener's learning curve is notoriously steep. New users need to watch hours of tutorials to master all its features. Its interface design is stuck a decade in the past, many operations require multiple clicks, and the mobile experience is frustrating. Lattics rethinks the creative software experience: [...] Zero Learning Curve: Intuitive from the start, the interface feels like physical cards.
 Unified Workspace: No need to switch between an editor, outliner, and research area.
 Smart Organization: Cards are automatically categorized and support multiple filtering options like tags, colors, and statuses.
 Cross-Platform Sync: Seamless connection between desktop and mobile, so inspiration can be recorded anytime.
 Modern Design Language: A clean interface, smooth animations, and a pleasant visual experience. [...] #### Conclusion: A New Era for Writing Tools

Scrivener is undoubtedly a landmark piece of writing software that has redefined the workflow for many authors. But over a decade has passed, and both creative methods and the technological landscape have changed. Lattics represents the next direction for writing tools: more intuitive, more visual, and more aligned with the non-linear nature of human thought. If you feel your current writing tools are limiting your creative process, if you are tired of constantly switching between folders and documents, if you want a tool that lets you truly "see" the whole picture of your story—then Lattics is worth a try.

results.title: Does Lattics Have What It Takes To Make It As a New Writing Software App?
results.content:
If you need more or want the export option, then an upgrade to Pro would be needed at $20.99/year or $3.99/month.

### Lattics has the building blocks it needs but it isn’t yet there as a fully featured app

Yes, Lattics is still buggy, but it’s one to keep your eye on. They have a powerful framework under the hood, which if optimised (maybe for academics) would rival the best writing apps.

#### Here’s the TL;DR review summary:

#### Will I continue using it?

Maybe. I will keep a close eye on its progress. I have a very strong system in place at the moment and not one I’m ready to change just yet.

Promisingly they are updating the app all the time. Since I installed the software, they’ve added typewriter and focus modes which I have yet to try. [...] ### Lets review Lattics

#### What does the software actually do and where does it sit in the market?

Lattics is a block-based writing application but unlike other block-based ones, I’m talking about you Notion, it’s much more subtle and doesn’t ruin the flow of writing. I thought this would turn me off straight away, but it does make editing and moving things around easier.

Lets also be clear what it is not. It is not a task-based application (although there is the option for tickable boxes). There is no calendar or time management options. It is purely for writing. The most similar program I have used to it is Scrivener. There is some Bear-esque features in there, though having never used Bear, I’m guessing. [...] Writing/formatting is as I would expect. The design templates are a great idea. I feel they could be onto a winner with these, especially if you can set up your own. I like how they have thought about research journal templates. This is an indication that the developers have one eye on the use of this app by academics. Cool stuff.

I also like that they are making LaTeX a part of it, another sign of a more academic angle. Though as yet, I can’t use a $ without invoking LaTeX code... I’d love to see Zotero properly integrated with the use of a bibliography-building function using bib-keys. At the moment, it just acts as a link to the Zotero file.

results.title: Lattics | Its Function “Sucks”, But The Brain Helps
results.content:
Although they provided a Block, it’s a Block with chaotic logic and incomplete functionality. It appears that the Lattics team hasn’t used popular block-based note-taking software like Notion, Heptabase, Capacities, or Feishu. We are also unsure whether there is anyone in the team who writes frequently.

There are also some minor issues. For example, when we change a line to a title by using “#”, we cannot revert it to plain text by pressing “Backspace” at the beginning of the line, nor can we select the title to transform it back to text. Instead, we can only click the six dots at the beginning of the line and then “convert” it to text. [...] Faced with these three views, I’m not quite sure if I’m operating correctly. If you’ve previously used Obsidian’s Canvas, Heptabase, or Scrintal, you might find Lattics confusing. You won’t clearly know which type of view you “should” be using, or if you are “correctly” using the current view.

## It Helps

Although Lattics may not be an excellent note-taking software, it is actually quite good for writing.

Lattics is particularly suitable for “traditional” people — those who have difficulty understanding new things or are reluctant to change their long-established writing habits using local software like Word.

Lattics still retains the retro love for magazines. In its document styles, it offers three magazine-style options: The Economist, Brooklyn, and Medium. [...] Instead, we have to first drag it to the page and then again click “Quote Full Content” to quote the full content.

Press enter or click to view image in full sizeImage 15

Press enter or click to view image in full sizeImage 16

We can see that all operations during input require the participation of the mouse, and it’s cumbersome. The Lattics team seems to have misunderstandings regarding the logic of paragraphs, text, and blocks, along with the use of the mouse and keyboard input. During writing, this strong logical conflict is palpable, hindering our writing and editing process.

results.title: Why Choose Lattics Over Obsidian? | Lattics Review
results.content:
In the following cases, it is highly recommended that you try Lattics:

1. You are a project-driven creator: Writing a book, script, long report, or academic paper, and need a full-process tool from outline, research, writing, to formatting.
2. You are a visual thinker: Accustomed to using timelines, charts, and mind maps to plan and sort out content structure, not just bidirectional links.
3. You are an academic researcher or student: You want a writing tool that can natively and smoothly handle references and citations without complex configuration.
4. You pursue "out-of-the-box" efficiency: You don't want to spend a lot of time selecting, testing, and configuring plugins, and desire a beautifully designed, highly integrated tool that can be put into production immediately. [...] | Onboarding & Learning Curve | The interface is intuitive, and features are highly integrated, making it easier for new users to get started and quickly use it for actual projects. | Powerful but relatively scattered features; realizing its full potential requires exploring and installing numerous plugins, leading to a steep learning curve. | For users who want an "out-of-the-box" experience to quickly get into the creative state, the Lattics experience is more friendly and smooth. |
